criteria.evidence: For the supplied observation-window records, the West/East aggregate incident-rate disparity is explained by the recorded severity composition: the regions' incident rates match within both listed severity strata, while West routed proportionally more high-severity items. These records do not establish vendor underperformance, staffing-transition causation, or grounds for sanction.

The records do not measure possible confounders within the high- and low-severity strata, and they do not show whether the staffing transition affected which items were routed to West; finer severity or case-mix differences could therefore remain.

criteria.evidence: The West vendor staffing transition degraded incident handling during the observation window, causing West's higher aggregate incident rate independently of routed-item severity.

The aggregate regional disparity is caused by different severity mixes: West received a much larger share of high-severity items, while West and East had the same incident rate within each recorded severity stratum.

criteria.evidence: A zero or negligible maximum within-severity West/East rate gap would support the severity-mix rival and weigh against staffing-related degradation independent of severity. A substantial gap, especially one consistently favoring East, would support the primary hypothesis over severity mix alone, although it would not by itself prove that the staffing transition caused the gap.
